# Medical Education

Research and news on artificial intelligence in medical education.

Items are selected nightly by an automated pipeline and link to their original sources. See [About](../about.md) for how selection works.

<img class="section-banner" src="../../assets/section-medical-education.svg" alt="">

<div class="section-brief">
<p>Significant gaps exist between medical students&#x27; strong demand for artificial intelligence training and the amount of instruction currently available, as documented in a cross-sectional study at a Turkish medical faculty <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42381025?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260701121028&amp;v=2.20.0">[2]</a>. Similar readiness deficits are found among Korean medical students, who lack sufficient preparation to use artificial intelligence effectively in their training <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42376791?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260630132631&amp;v=2.20.0">[6]</a>. Meanwhile, U.S. healthcare degree programs are rapidly integrating artificial intelligence, yet the process varies widely across institutions <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42370260?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260629131807&amp;v=2.20.0">[9]</a>. Studies also highlight technical limitations, such as persistent sex and racial biases in images generated by text-to-image artificial intelligence models for critical care physician representation <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42376226?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260630092944&amp;v=2.20.0">[3]</a>.</p>
<p class="section-brief-date">The picture as of July 1, 2026; numbered links go to the items below.</p>
</div>

    <p class="news-card-summary">Prospective evaluation shows large language models possess solid ophthalmic knowledge and can effectively support ophthalmology training in China.</p>
